text:
Problem Overview

In this problem, students continue to convert ratios to percent statements as a way to compare the ratios. By analyzing student work in What If...?, students are introduced to tape diagrams (also called percent bars when using them to analyze percents) and double number lines. Students also look at finding percent amounts using strategies that they have used in previous investigations, such as scaling and rate tables.

Implementation Note: You will want to have a conversation after the Initial Challenge before moving on to the What If...?. This will allow you to highlight your students' strategies before analyzing the strategies of other students. You can use the strategies presented i nWhat If...? Situation A to connect to how your students though about the percentages in the surbery questions. Then, use What If...? Situations B and C to assess how students can use percent thinking to make sense of a new situation. This problem will take 1.5 days to complete.

Arc of Learning: Exploration, Analysis

Standards: See your state alignment chart.

NWDYK?

How are diagrams such as percent bars (tape diagrams)or double number lines helpful for answering percent questions? What other strategies might be helpful?

Emerging Mathematical Ideas

  • Make connections between and among a variety of strategies for solving problems involving percents.
  • Look closely at a variety of percent problem-solving strategies to notice that
    • tape diagrams and double number lines allow you to easily visualize the size of a percentage;
    • ratio tables, unit rate, scaling, and 10 × 10 area grids can each be useful in solving some rate problems; and
    • while many strategies may be used to solve percent problems, some may be more or less efficiently applied than others and all strategies should be considered.

    Solve a variety of percent problems, including those asking students to find

    • the percent give a ratio
    • the quantity in the whole given the ratio and amount in the part of the whole; and
    • the quantity that is the part of the whole given the percent and amount in the whole.

Launch (Getting Started)

Connecting to Prior Knowledge

Remind students of the percent bar from Problem 4.1. Ask students to summarize how the bar was helpful for finding a percent.
Introduce the vocabulary of tape diagram as another term for percent bar if you didn't introduce it during the summary of Problem 4.1.

Presenting the Challenge

Introduce the survey questions from Mr. Satyam's class and results to the students. TO familiarize students with the context, you might do a quick survey in the class.

  • What is your favorite lunch? How many of you like tacos for school lunch?
  • What is your favorite class in school? How many of you would pick science?
  • Would you prefer wearing a school uniform or just everyday clothes to school?

Have students begin their analysis of the survey data independently and then move into talking with a partner to compare their thinking.
